title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
description: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time.
hints: Use Union-Find but only union consecutive numbers (num and num-1).
Put all numbers into a set first, then iterate and union each number with num-1 if it exists.
Track the size of each component and return the maximum.

title: Graph Valid Tree
description: You are given a list of n integers where edges[i] = [ai, bi] represents an undirected edge between node ai and node bi. Given n and a list of edges, return true if the given edges make up a valid tree, and false otherwise. A valid tree is connected and has no cycles.
hints: A valid tree with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges.
The graph must be connected: all nodes are in one component.
Use Union-Find: if any edge connects two already-connected nodes, there is a cycle.

title: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
description: You have a graph of n nodes labeled from 0 to n - 1. You are given an integer n and an array edges where edges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is an undirected edge between nodes ai and bi. Return the number of connected components in the graph.
hints: Each node starts in its own component.
For each edge, union the two nodes they connect.
The number of components is the number of nodes that are their own parent after all unions.

title: Number of Provinces
description: There are n cities. Some of them are connected, while some are not. If city a is connected directly with city b, and city b is connected directly with city c, then city a is connected indirectly with city c. A province is a group of directly or indirectly connected cities and no other cities outside of the group. You are given an n x n matrix isConnected where isConnected[i][j] = 1 if the ith city and the jth city are directly connected, and isConnected[i][j] = 0 otherwise. Return the number of provinces.
hints: Think of this as a graph problem where each city is a node.
Use Union-Find to group connected cities into components.
The answer is the number of distinct root nodes.

title: Redundant Connection
description: In this problem, a tree is an undirected graph that is connected and has no cycles. You are given a graph that started as a tree with n nodes labeled from 1 to n, with one additional edge added. The added edge has two different vertices chosen from 1 to n, and was not an edge that already existed. The graph is represented as an array edges of length n where edges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is an edge between nodes ai and bi in the graph. Return an edge that can be removed so that the resulting graph is a tree of n nodes. If there are multiple answers, return the answer that occurs last in the given input.
hints: A tree with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges.
Use Union-Find: if two nodes are already in the same set when trying to union them, that edge is redundant.
Process edges in order and return the first edge that connects two already-connected nodes.

title: Accounts Merge
description: Given a list of accounts where each element accounts[i] is a list of strings, where the first element accounts[i][0] is a name, and the rest of the elements are emails representing emails of the account. Now, we would like to merge these accounts. Two accounts definitely belong to the same person if there is some common email to both accounts. Note that even if two accounts have the same name, they may belong to different people as people could have the same name. A person can have any number of accounts initially, but all of their accounts definitely have the same name. After merging the accounts, return the accounts in the following format: the first element of each account is the name, and the rest of the elements are emails in sorted order. The accounts themselves can be returned in any order.
hints: Build a graph where nodes are emails and connect emails within the same account.
Alternatively, use Union-Find to merge accounts that share a common email.
After merging, group all emails by their root representative and attach the account name.

title: Satisfiability of Equality Equations
description: You are given an array of strings equations where each string is of length 4 and takes one of the following forms: 'a==b' to denote an equality, or 'a!=b' to denote an inequality. Return true if it is possible to assign integers to all variables such that all the given equations are satisfied.
hints: First process all equality equations and union the variables.
Then check all inequality equations: if two variables are in the same set, return false.
There are only 26 possible variables (a-z), so Union-Find is very efficient here.

title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
description: You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi]. The cost of connecting two points [xi, yi] and [xj, yj] is the Manhattan distance between them: |xi - xj| + |yi - yj|. Return the minimum cost to make all points connected. All points are connected if there is exactly one simple path between any two points.
hints: This is the Minimum Spanning Tree problem.
Use Kruskal's algorithm: sort all edges by weight and greedily add them with Union-Find.
Calculate all pairwise Manhattan distances, sort them, and process with Union-Find.

title: Number of Islands II
description: You are given an empty 2D binary grid grid of size m x n which represents a topographical view where land and water cells are represented by 1 and 0 respectively. A cell is part of the island if it has a 1 in its adjacent cells (up, down, left, right). You are also given a list of operations where operations[i] = [ri, ci] flips grid[ri][ci] from 0 to 1 (adding a land cell). Return an array of the number of islands after each operation.
hints: Use Union-Find to merge adjacent land cells as they are added.
Each new land cell starts as its own component; check all 4 neighbors and union if they are land.
Track the count of components and update it after each operation.
timeComplexity: O(k * alpha(m*n)) where k = number of operations

title: Redundant Connection II
description: In this problem, a rooted tree is one of the following acyclic connected directed graphs: a single vertex, or a rooted tree made with two or more vertices and two types of edges: directed edges and undirected edges. Every pair of vertices is connected by at most one simple path. You are given a directed graph that started as a rooted tree with n nodes labeled from 1 to n, with one additional edge added. The added edge has two different vertices chosen from 1 to n. The graph is represented as an array edges of length n where edges[i] = [ui, vi] indicates that there is a directed edge from ui to vi in the graph. Return an edge that can be removed so that the resulting graph is a rooted tree of n nodes. If there are multiple answers, return the answer that occurs last in the given input.
hints: A rooted tree with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges and every node except root has exactly one incoming edge.

Three cases: (1) one node has indegree 2 and there is a cycle - remove the duplicate incoming edge, (2) one node has indegree 2 and no cycle - remove the other edge, (3) no node has indegree 2 - remove the edge that creates the cycle.
Use Union-Find on undirected version to detect cycles, and track indegrees to identify the two candidates.
